content.problemStatement:
Great! You have successfully built a **URL Shortener** using Python. 🎉

Here are some improvements you can try:


**Store URLs Persistently** – Save shortened URLs in a database or a file to retain them after restarting the program.
**Implement API & Server** – Use Flask or FastAPI to create a web-based URL shortener service.
**Analytics & Tracking** – Track the number of times a short URL has been accessed.
**Web Interface** – Build a simple frontend using Flask or Django for a user-friendly experience.
**Final Updates:**
Ensure both **shortening** and **retrieval** functions work properly.
Handle edge cases like duplicate URLs and invalid inputs.
Improve user experience with clear messages and validations.
Try implementing these improvements to make your URL Shortener more efficient and user-friendly! 🚀
constraints: All URLs contain only printable ASCII characters except whitespace and have length ≤ 100.
Short codes consist of characters 0‑9, a‑z, A‑Z and have length ≤ 6.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer Q (1 ≤ Q ≤ 1000), the number of queries. The following Q lines each contain a query in one of two formats:
1. SHORTEN <url>   – shorten the given URL (the URL is a non‑empty string without spaces, length ≤ 100).
2. GET <code>      – retrieve the original URL for the given short code (the code consists of alphanumeric characters, length ≤ 6).
content.outputFormat:
For each query, output a single line:
- For a SHORTEN query, output the generated short code. If the URL has been shortened before, output the previously generated code.
- For a GET query, output the original URL if the code exists, otherwise output the word "error".

sampleTestCases.input:
7
SHORTEN https://example.com
SHORTEN https://openai.com
GET 1
GET 2
SHORTEN https://example.com
GET 1
GET 2
sampleTestCases.explanation: The first SHORTEN creates code "1" for https://example.com. The second creates "2" for https://openai.com. GET 1 and GET 2 are asked before any GET, but codes "1" and "2" exist, so they return the URLs. The third SHORTEN is a duplicate URL, so it reuses code "1". The final GET queries retrieve the stored URLs.

sampleTestCases.input:
5
GET abc
SHORTEN https://test.org
GET 1
SHORTEN https://test.org
GET 1
sampleTestCases.explanation: GET abc fails because no such code exists. The first SHORTEN generates code "1". GET 1 now returns the stored URL. The second SHORTEN is a duplicate, so it returns the same code "1". The final GET again returns the URL.

sampleTestCases.input:
4
SHORTEN a
SHORTEN b
SHORTEN c
GET 3
sampleTestCases.explanation: Three distinct URLs receive sequential codes 1, 2 and 3. GET 3 retrieves the third URL "c".

solution.approach:
We maintain two hash maps:
- url2code: maps a full URL to its short code.
- code2url: maps a short code back to the original URL.
When a SHORTEN query arrives we check url2code. If the URL already exists we reuse its code; otherwise we generate a new code by converting an increasing integer id to base‑62 (characters 0‑9, a‑z, A‑Z). The new mapping is stored in both hash maps.
For a GET query we look up the code in code2url; if it is present we output the stored URL, otherwise we output "error".
Both operations are O(1) average time using hash tables. The total time per test case is O(Q) and the memory consumption is O(number of distinct URLs).
solution.timeComplexity: O(Q) per test case (each query is O(1) average).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) where N is the number of distinct URLs stored (at most Q).
